title: Why success and achievement can trigger imposter collapse
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
The phone call that broke me open 

 I was thirty-one years old. I had just been promoted to a position I'd fought for, clawed for, cried over for three y...
htmlContent:
The phone call that broke me open

I was thirty-one years old. I had just been promoted to a position I'd fought for, clawed for, cried over for three years. My salary doubled. My title became something I could say at dinner parties without cringing. And I sat in my car in the parking garage, hands shaking, unable to open the door.

My mother called. I answered. She said "I'm so proud of you, honey." And I started sobbing. Not happy tears. Something else. Something that felt like my chest was caving in. I hung up and sat there for forty-five minutes, staring at the concrete wall, wondering why success felt like being buried alive.

That was my first real encounter with what I now call imposter collapse. Not imposter syndrome, where you doubt your abilities and feel like a fraud. That I could handle. That I was used to. This was different. This was my nervous system hitting a wall so hard that my entire sense of self shattered. Right at the moment when everything was supposed to feel good.

Here's the thing. Our culture tells us that success heals. That achievement is the antidote to shame. That if we just get enough gold stars, enough promotions, enough external validation, we will finally feel safe inside our own skin. But that's a lie. A dangerous one.

For those of us with complex trauma histories, success can be the most triggering thing we ever experience. Not because we don't deserve it. Not because we're frauds. But because our nervous systems learned a different set of rules about what safety looks like. And achievement, real visible achievement, breaks those rules.

The nervous system doesn't care about your resume

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about something called neuroception. It's the way your nervous system scans for safety and danger below conscious awareness. Your brain doesn't check your LinkedIn profile before deciding if you're safe. It checks your body. Your breathing. Your muscle tension. The faces of the people around you.

When you grow up in an environment where visibility was dangerous, where being seen meant being attacked, criticized, or abandoned, your nervous system learns a survival strategy. Stay small. Stay quiet. Don't stand out. Blend in. This is not a conscious choice. It's a biological adaptation. Your body learned that invisibility equals safety.

Then you go out into the world and you achieve. You get the job. You get the award. You get the promotion. People look at you. They applaud. They expect things. And your nervous system, which never got the memo that you're now an adult in a different environment, screams DANGER. We are visible. We are exposed. We are not safe.

This is imposter collapse. It's not a thought. It's a physiological response. Your heart races. Your chest tightens. Your mind goes blank. You feel like you're going to be found out, but not because you lack skills. Because your body remembers what happened the last time you were seen.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, would say that this is the body completing a survival response that got interrupted. When you were small and visibility meant danger, you froze. You dissociated. You learned to hide. Now, as an adult, success activates that same freeze response. But you can't hide anymore. You're in the spotlight. And your body doesn't know what to do with that contradiction.

I remember reading Levine's work and feeling like someone had finally described my internal experience. The panic wasn't about being exposed as a fraud. The panic was about being exposed, period. Full stop. My body didn't distinguish between being seen for a presentation and being seen by an angry parent. It just knew that being seen was not safe.

Why the wellness industry fails us here

This is where I need to be direct with you. The wellness industry has failed people like us. Badly. They tell us to affirm ourselves. To visualize success. To our dreams. To just believe we deserve it. And none of that works because it's aimed at the cognitive brain, not the nervous system.

You can tell yourself "I deserve this promotion" a thousand times. Your body will still shake. Your throat will still close. Your stomach will still knot up. Because affirmations don't reach the part of your brain that runs your survival responses. That part doesn't speak English. It speaks sensation. It speaks vibration. It speaks safety.

I spent years trying to think my way out of this collapse. I read every self-help book. I journaled. I did the gratitude lists. And every time I achieved something real, something meaningful, I ended up in the bathroom at work, splashing water on my face, wondering why I couldn't just feel good about my life.

Here's what I wish someone had told me. Your imposter collapse is not a sign that you don't belong. It's a sign that your nervous system is trying to protect you using old strategies that no longer apply. Your job is not to convince yourself you're worthy. Your job is to teach your body that visibility is safe now.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg. It helps take the edge off that hypervigilant alertness without making you foggy. It's not a cure. Nothing is a cure. But it can create enough space in your nervous system to start doing the real work.

The double bind of achievement

Here's another layer that makes this so painful. Many of us with complex trauma also learned that our worth was conditional on achievement. We had to be perfect to be loved. We had to succeed to be safe. So we became high achievers. We overfunctioned. We burned ourselves out trying to earn a sense of belonging that was never offered freely.

Then we achieve. And it doesn't give us what we thought it would. The love doesn't come. The safety doesn't arrive. We still feel empty. And now we're terrified because if achievement doesn't fix us, then what will? What's left?

This is the double bind. You need to achieve to feel worthy. But achieving triggers your nervous system. And when it doesn't heal you, you feel even more broken. You feel like there's something wrong with you that no amount of success can fix. And that's a terrible place to be.

Janina Fisher, who writes about structural dissociation and trauma treatment, talks about how parts of us can be stuck in different time periods. One part of you is the high-achieving adult who knows how to succeed. Another part is the terrified child who learned that being seen is dangerous. These parts don't communicate well. They're running on different operating systems. And when success happens, they collide.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate. Magnesium is involved in over three hundred biochemical reactions in your body, including the regulation of your stress response. Many trauma survivors are deficient. Taking it before bed can help your nervous system settle enough to process what's happening.

But supplements alone won't untangle this knot. You need to understand that your achievement drive and your collapse response are two sides of the same coin. They both came from the same wound. And healing means finding a way to let both parts exist without letting either one run the show.

What recovery actually looks like

I'm not going to give you a five-step plan. That would be dishonest. Recovery from imposter collapse is messy, nonlinear, and deeply personal. But I can tell you what has helped me and what I've seen help others.

First, you have to stop fighting your nervous system. When the collapse comes, when you feel that wave of panic or shame or dissociation, your instinct is to push it away. To tell yourself you're being ridiculous. To power through. That makes it worse. Your nervous system needs to be met with compassion, not rejection.

Second, you need to learn to track your body's signals before the collapse hits. For me, it's a tightness in my jaw and a feeling of unreality, like I'm watching myself from outside my body. For you, it might be something different. But if you can catch it early, you can intervene before the full collapse happens.

Third, you need to find ways to let your body know that visibility is safe now. This is not about affirmations. It's about small, repeated experiences of being seen and not being harmed. Maybe that means sharing something vulnerable with a trusted friend and noticing that they don't attack you. Maybe it means standing in front of a mirror and just looking at yourself without judgment for thirty seconds. Maybe it means letting someone take your picture and not immediately hating it.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker. He writes about the inner critic, the emotional flashbacks, and the specific ways that achievement can trigger collapse in trauma survivors. It's not a quick fix. But it's a map. And sometimes a map is what you need when you're lost in the dark.

Fourth, you have to grieve. This is the hard part. You have to grieve that success didn't save you. That all those years of striving didn't earn you the love you deserved. That the child you were didn't get what they needed, and no amount of adult achievement can go back and give it to them. Grief is not a step you complete. It's a process you return to. Again and again.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs. When your nervous system is on high alert, sound can be overwhelming. These reduce the volume of the world without blocking it out entirely. Sometimes you need to lower the input to hear your own signal.

I'm not going to tell you that you'll never feel imposter collapse again. I still feel it. I felt it when I published my first article. I felt it when I got my first speaking invitation. I felt it when someone called me an expert and I wanted to crawl under my desk. But it's different now. It doesn't own me. I can feel the panic rising and say "Oh, there you are. I know you. You're trying to protect me. Thank you. But we're okay now."

That's the goal. Not to eliminate the response. To build enough capacity in your nervous system that you can hold it without being destroyed by it. To let success and visibility exist alongside the fear, without the fear taking over.

Sit with that. Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did your chest tighten? Did your shoulders relax? Did you want to stop reading? All of that is information. Your nervous system is talking to you. It's been talking to you this whole time. You just didn't know how to listen.

You're not broken. You're not a fraud. You're a person whose nervous system learned to survive in a world that wasn't safe. And now you're learning that the world has changed. That visibility doesn't have to mean danger. That achievement can be yours without costing you everything.

It's slow work. It's hard work. But it's possible. I promise you it's possible.
